food_name: Toor Dal (Split Pigeon Peas, Raw, Dried)

local_names.romanized_local: Toor Dal / Arhar Dal / Tuvar Dal / Thuvaram Paruppu / Togari Bele

aliases: Toor Dal / Split Pigeon Peas (raw, dried)

english: Toor Dal / Split Pigeon Peas (raw, dried)

food_subgroup: Split pigeon peas (Cajanus cajan), dried raw

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pigeon pea concept (FOODON_03316089) but no specific entry for split dried toor dal. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pigeon peas (Cajanus cajan), split (toor/arhar dal), dried raw
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A037M covers pigeon peas broadly; no dedicated split-dried sub-code confirmed. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No confirmed SNOMED CT code for split dried toor dal. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard dry serving of toor dal is approximately 50g (about 3.5 tablespoons), which yields roughly 100–120g of cooked dal after pressure cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Toor Dal / Split Pigeon Peas; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Pigeon peas, raw)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for toor dal (raw, dried). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pigeon peas raw (FDC ID 175237) for micronutrient confirmation. Values are consistent across both sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Pigeon peas / toor dal, boiled
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of approximately 32 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled toor/pigeon peas. The GI applies to the cooked form; raw dried toor dal does not have a directly measurable GI. GL per 50g dry serving calculated as: (32 × 57.6 × 0.5) / 100 = 9.2. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high potassium (1392mg/100g) and magnesium (183mg/100g) with very low sodium (17mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Toor dal's outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio (82:1) and high magnesium content directly support vascular tone and blood pressure regulation, making it one of the most beneficial legumes for hypertensive patients when consumed as part of a low-sodium preparation such as sambar without added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~32 boiled), high dietary fibre (15g/100g dry), and high protein (22.3g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The very low GI (~32) of cooked toor dal, combined with 15g dietary fibre and 22.3g protein per 100g dry weight, produces a slow, blunted postprandial glucose response, making it a clinically appropriate staple for Type 2 Diabetes management, particularly when served with high-fibre accompaniments such as brown rice or ragi idli.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble fibre, very low saturated fat (0.4g/100g), zero cholesterol, and high folate (456mcg DFE/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Toor dal's negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high soluble dietary fibre, and exceptional folate content (456mcg DFE/100g) support LDL reduction and homocysteine regulation, making it a cardioprotective legume suitable for daily consumption in cardiovascular disease prevention and management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate (456mcg DFE), potassium, magnesium, and anti-inflammatory plant proteins
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Toor dal provides high folate (456mcg DFE/100g dry), potassium, and magnesium alongside anti-inflammatory plant proteins, supporting joint health and potentially moderating inflammatory markers in rheumatoid arthritis patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Toor dal (also called arhar dal or tuvar dal) is split pigeon peas (Cajanus cajan) — the most widely consumed dal in South and Western India and the foundational ingredient for sambar, the quintessential South Indian lentil soup. The split peas are pale yellow, slightly larger than moong dal, with a mildly earthy and robust flavour that holds up well to the tamarind, tomato, and spice-heavy sambar preparation. Toor dal requires longer cooking than moong dal; pressure cooking for 15–20 minutes is standard. It is also used in Gujarati dal (sweet-sour), dal fry, dal tadka, and khichdi. Nutritionally, toor dal is exceptional: it contains 335 kcal, 22.3g protein, 57.6g carbohydrate, and only 1.7g fat per 100g dry weight. It is extraordinarily high in potassium (1392mg/100g) — the highest among common South Indian dals — making it outstanding for blood pressure management. It is also very high in folate (456mcg DFE/100g), magnesium (183mg), phosphorus (367mg), and dietary fibre (15g). The glycemic index of cooked toor dal is very low (~32), making it clinically beneficial for Type 2 Diabetes management. Cholesterol is zero and saturated fat is minimal (0.4g). Toor dal is a staple in Karnataka (togari bele), Tamil Nadu (thuvaram paruppu), Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh, and Gujarat cuisines. It is vegan, gluten-free, and halal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (primary approved source for Indian foods) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Both sources are consistent. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for toor dal not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA SR Legacy at the level of individual MUFA/PUFA; total fat (1.7g) is confirmed. water_g set to -1: moisture content for raw dried toor dal not reported in the primary ICMR-NIN source consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant legume with no industrial hydrogenation). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy for raw dried toor dal). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant legume). GI value of 32 applies to the cooked (boiled/pressure-cooked) form; raw dried dal does not have a directly measurable GI. GL per 50g dry serving = (32 × 57.6 × 0.5) / 100 = 9.2. Folate reported as mcg DFE per ICMR-NIN. Vitamin A reported as 1 mcg RAE (trace). Selenium 8.2 mcg per USDA SR Legacy cross-reference.
